\documentclass[american]{article}
\usepackage[T1]{fontenc}
\usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}
\usepackage{geometry}
\geometry{verbose,tmargin=1cm,bmargin=1cm,lmargin=1cm,rmargin=1cm}
\usepackage{calc}
\usepackage{amsmath}
\usepackage{amssymb}
\usepackage{babel}
\begin{document}

\subsection{Problem Setup}

Consider the simple linear model with Gaussian data and white Gaussian
noise, data generative model
\begin{equation}
y=\beta^{T}\mathbf{x}+\ \sigma\epsilon\label{eq:linear_model_gauss_noise}
\end{equation}

Consider $n$ data points, with $d$ dimension. 

Data points $\mathbf{x}$ has population covariance $\Sigma$ (assume
to be zero mean at this moment).  
\[
X=[\mathbf{x}^{(1)}|\mathbf{x}^{(2)}|...|\mathbf{x}^{(n)}]^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}
\]

$\eta$ denote the realized noise with unit variance. 

\subsection{Coefficient estimation error along dataset PC }

Consider the OLS estimator, 
\begin{align*}
\hat{\beta} & =X^{\dagger}\mathbf{y}\\
 & =(X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}\mathbf{y}\\
 & =(X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}(X\beta^{*}+\sigma\eta)\\
 & =(X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}X\beta^{*}+\sigma(X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}\eta\\
 & =\beta^{*}+\sigma(X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}\eta
\end{align*}

More generally, consider Ridge estimator 
\begin{align*}
\hat{\beta} & =(X^{T}X+n\lambda I_{d})^{-1}X^{T}\mathbf{y}\\
 & =(X^{T}X+n\lambda I_{d})^{-1}X^{T}(X\beta^{*}+\sigma\eta)\\
 & =(X^{T}X+n\lambda I_{d})^{-1}X^{T}X\beta^{*}+\sigma(X^{T}X+n\lambda I_{d})^{-1}X^{T}\eta\\
 & =(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}\beta^{*}+\sigma(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)
\end{align*}

We can consider the deviation of the weight from the optimal one,
using abbreviation $\hat{\Sigma}:=\frac{1}{n}X^{T}X$, and rewrite
the samples as $X=Z\Sigma^{1/2}$
\begin{align*}
(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}) & =\Big((X^{T}X+n\lambda I_{d})^{-1}X^{T}X-I\Big)\beta^{*}+\sigma(X^{T}X+n\lambda I_{d})^{-1}X^{\top}\eta\\
 & =\Big((\frac{1}{n}X^{T}X+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\frac{1}{n}X^{T}X-I\Big)\beta^{*}+\sigma(\frac{1}{n}X^{T}X+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta\\
 & =\Big((\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}-I\Big)\beta^{*}+\sigma(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\big(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta\big)\\
 & =-\lambda(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\beta^{*}+\sigma(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\Sigma^{1/2}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\big)\\
 & =-\lambda(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\beta^{*}+\sigma(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\Sigma^{1/2}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\big)
\end{align*}

Note that the 2nd term $\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\big)$ it's $d$
dim vector, where each entry is the average of $n$ i.i.d. product
normal variable. At the large n limit it will have variance=1. 
\begin{align*}
\Delta\beta_{noise} & =\sigma(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\Sigma^{1/2}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\big)\\
 & \approx\sigma(\Sigma+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\Sigma^{1/2}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\big)
\end{align*}

along each population eigen directions $\mathbf{u}_{k}$. 
\[
\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}\Delta\beta_{noise}\approx\sigma\frac{\sqrt{\lambda_{k}}}{\lambda+\lambda_{k}}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\big)
\]

We can say
\[
X=Z\Sigma^{1/2}
\]

Using deterministic equivalence we have
\[
(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*})\asymp\Big((\Sigma+\kappa\big(\lambda\big)I_{d})^{-1}\Sigma-I\Big)\beta^{*}+\sigma(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\Sigma^{1/2}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\big)
\]

We could consider the expectation of the deviation along an PC
\[
\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*})\asymp-\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{k}+\kappa}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}\beta^{*}+
\]

We could consider the expectation of the squared error on each direction,
the expectation is taken over realization of data $X$ (or equivalently
$Z$) and noise $\eta$ 
\begin{align*}
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[(\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}\Big] & =\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*})^{\top}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*})\Big]\\
 & =\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\lambda^{2}\beta^{*\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\beta^{*}\\
 & \quad\quad+\sigma^{2}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\big)^{\top}\Sigma^{1/2}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\Sigma^{1/2}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\big)\\
 & \quad\quad+-2\sigma\lambda\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\big)^{\top}\Sigma^{1/2}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\beta^{*}\Big]
\end{align*}

Taking expectation over the $\eta$, we have 
\begin{align*}
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[(\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}\Big] & =\mathbb{E}_{X}\Big[\lambda^{2}\beta^{*\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\beta^{*}\\
 & \quad\quad+\frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\Sigma^{1/2}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\eta^{\top}Z\big)\Sigma^{1/2}\Big]\\
 & =\mathbb{E}_{\hat{\Sigma}}\Big[\lambda^{2}\beta^{*\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\beta^{*}\\
 & \quad\quad+\frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}\text{Tr}\big[(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}\big]\Big]
\end{align*}

We have 
\begin{align*}
\mathbb{E}_{\eta}\Big[\Sigma^{1/2}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}\eta\eta^{\top}Z\big)\Sigma^{1/2}\Big] & =\Sigma^{1/2}\big(\frac{1}{n}Z^{\top}I_{n}Z\big)\Sigma^{1/2}\\
 & =\frac{1}{n}\Sigma^{1/2}Z^{\top}Z\Sigma^{1/2}\\
 & =\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}X\\
 & =\hat{\Sigma}
\end{align*}

We could apply deterministic equivalence here 
\begin{align*}
(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1} & =(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}-\lambda(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-2}\\
 & =\frac{d}{d\lambda}\Big(\lambda(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\Big)
\end{align*}

So 2nd term reads
\begin{align*}
 & \mathbb{E}_{\hat{\Sigma}}\Big[\frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}\text{Tr}\big[(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}\big]\Big]\\
= & \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}\mathbb{E}_{\hat{\Sigma}}\Big[\frac{d}{d\lambda}\Big(\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}\lambda(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\Big)\Big]\\
\asymp & \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}\mathbb{E}_{\hat{\Sigma}}\Big[\frac{d}{d\lambda}\Big(\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}\kappa(\Sigma+\kappa I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\Big)\Big]\\
= & \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}\frac{d}{d\lambda}\frac{\kappa(\lambda)}{\kappa(\lambda)+\lambda_{k}}\\
= & \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}(\frac{d\kappa}{d\lambda})\frac{d}{d\kappa}\frac{\kappa(\lambda)}{\kappa(\lambda)+\lambda_{k}}\\
= & \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}\frac{d\kappa}{d\lambda}\frac{\lambda_{k}}{(\kappa+\lambda_{k})^{2}}
\end{align*}

Using
\[
\frac{d}{d\kappa}\left(\frac{\kappa}{\kappa+\lambda_{k}}\right)=\frac{\lambda_{k}}{(\kappa+\lambda_{k})^{2}}
\]

The 1st term can be tackled by the two-point equivalence 
\[
\mbox{Tr}\bigl[A\,(\widehat{\Sigma}+zI)^{-1}\bigr]\sim\frac{\kappa(z)}{z}\mbox{Tr}\bigl[A\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(z)I\bigr)^{-1}\bigr]
\]
\begin{align*}
\mbox{Tr}\bigl[A(\widehat{\Sigma}+zI)^{-1}B(\widehat{\Sigma}+zI)^{-1}\bigr] & \sim\frac{\kappa(z)^{2}}{z^{2}\,}\mbox{Tr}\bigl[A\,\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(z)I\bigr)^{-1}\,B\,\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(z)I\bigr)^{-1}\bigr]\\
 & \quad+\frac{\kappa(z)^{2}}{z^{2}}\frac{1}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa(z)\bigr)}\mbox{Tr}\bigl[A\,\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(z)I\bigr)^{-2}\Sigma\bigr]\mbox{Tr}\bigl[B\,\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(z)I\bigr)^{-2}\Sigma\bigr]
\end{align*}

\begin{align*}
 & \mathbb{E}_{\hat{\Sigma}}\Big[\lambda^{2}\beta^{*\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\beta^{*}\Big]\\
\asymp & \kappa(\lambda)^{2}\mbox{Tr}\bigl[\beta^{*\top}\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(\lambda)I\bigr)^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(\lambda)I\bigr)^{-1}\beta^{*}\bigr]\\
 & +\frac{\kappa^{2}}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}\mbox{Tr}\bigl[\mathbf{u}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(\lambda)I\bigr)^{-2}\Sigma\bigr]\mbox{Tr}\bigl[\beta^{*}\beta^{*\top}\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(\lambda)I\bigr)^{-2}\Sigma\bigr]\\
= & \kappa^{2}(\beta^{*\top}\mathbf{u}_{k})^{2}\frac{1}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}\\
 & +\frac{\kappa^{2}}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}\frac{\lambda_{k}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}\Big(\beta^{*\top}\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(\lambda)I\bigr)^{-2}\Sigma\beta^{*}\Big)\\
= & \frac{\kappa^{2}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}(\beta^{*\top}\mathbf{u}_{k})^{2}+\frac{\kappa^{2}}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}\frac{\lambda_{k}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}\Big(\beta^{*\top}\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa(\lambda)I\bigr)^{-2}\Sigma\beta^{*}\Big)\\
= & \frac{\kappa^{2}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}(\beta^{*\top}\mathbf{u}_{k})^{2}+\frac{\kappa^{2}}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}\frac{\lambda_{k}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}\Big(\sum_{k}\frac{\lambda_{k}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}(\beta^{*\top}\mathbf{u}_{k})^{2}\Big)
\end{align*}

Taking together we have the formula for the squared deviation along
PCs. 
\begin{align*}
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[(\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}\Big] & \asymp\frac{\kappa^{2}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}(\beta^{*\top}\mathbf{u}_{k})^{2}\\
 & \quad+\frac{1}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}\frac{\lambda_{k}\kappa^{2}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}\Big(\beta^{*\top}\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa I\bigr)^{-2}\Sigma\beta^{*}\Big)\\
 & \quad+\frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}\frac{d\kappa}{d\lambda}\frac{\lambda_{k}}{(\kappa+\lambda_{k})^{2}}\\
 & \asymp\frac{\kappa^{2}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}(\beta^{*\top}\mathbf{u}_{k})^{2}\\
 & \quad+\frac{1}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}\frac{\lambda_{k}\kappa^{2}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}\Big(\beta^{*\top}\bigl(\Sigma+\kappa I\bigr)^{-2}\Sigma\beta^{*}\Big)\\
 & \quad+\frac{\sigma^{2}}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}\frac{\lambda_{k}}{(\kappa+\lambda_{k})^{2}}
\end{align*}

\noindent\begin{minipage}[t]{1\columnwidth}%
\begin{align*}
\frac{d\kappa}{d\lambda} & =(\frac{d\lambda}{d\kappa})^{-1}\\
 & =\frac{n}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}
\end{align*}
%
\end{minipage}

\noindent\fbox{\begin{minipage}[t]{1\columnwidth - 2\fboxsep - 2\fboxrule}%
\textbf{Derivation of $d\kappa/d\lambda$}

\[
\kappa(z)-z=\gamma\kappa(z)\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{sd\mu(s)}{\kappa(z)+s}
\]

in discrete case 
\begin{align*}
\kappa(\lambda)-\lambda & =\gamma\kappa(\lambda)\frac{1}{d}\sum_{i}\frac{s_{i}}{\kappa(\lambda)+s_{i}}\\
\lambda & =\kappa(\lambda)\Big[1-\gamma\frac{1}{d}\sum_{i}\frac{s_{i}}{\kappa(\lambda)+s_{i}}\Big]\\
\frac{d\lambda}{d\kappa} & =1-\gamma\frac{1}{d}\sum_{i}\frac{s_{i}}{\kappa(\lambda)+s_{i}}+\kappa(\lambda)\gamma\frac{1}{d}\sum_{i}\frac{s_{i}}{(\kappa(\lambda)+s_{i})^{2}}\\
 & =1+\gamma\frac{1}{d}\sum_{i}\frac{s_{i}\kappa(\lambda)-s_{i}(\kappa(\lambda)+s_{i})}{(\kappa(\lambda)+s_{i})^{2}}\\
 & =1-\gamma\frac{1}{d}\sum_{i}\frac{s_{i}^{2}}{(\kappa(\lambda)+s_{i})^{2}}\\
 & =1-\gamma\frac{1}{d}\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)
\end{align*}

$\gamma=d/n$ so 
\begin{align*}
\frac{d\lambda}{d\kappa} & =1-\frac{1}{n}\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)\\
\frac{d\kappa}{d\lambda} & =\frac{n}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}
\end{align*}
%
\end{minipage}}

We have three terms
\begin{itemize}
\item 1st term can be understood as ``overshrinkage'' of true weight due
to Ridge, 
\begin{itemize}
\item it's proportional to the true magnitude along this PC $(\beta^{*\top}\mathbf{u}_{k})^{2}$.
\item It will vanish at the limit (no Ridge) $\kappa^{2}\to0$
\item and when the $\lambda_{k}>\kappa$ this term is quite small, i.e.
Ridge will treat it as signal and learn it correctly. 
\item when $\kappa>\lambda_{k}$, Ridge will tend to treat it as noise and
shrink too much, leading to the error coef proportional to $\frac{\kappa^{2}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}$
\item This $\frac{\kappa^{2}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}\in(0,1)$ cannot
explode. 
\item This term is not finite sample effect, even exist in infinite sample. 
\end{itemize}
\item 2nd term is still about signal 
\begin{itemize}
\item It's a Ridge effect, it's proportional to $\kappa^{2}$ so will vanish
when no ridge. 
\item its anisotropic factor is like $\frac{\kappa^{2}\lambda_{k}}{(\lambda_{k}+\kappa)^{2}}$,
it will peak at $\lambda_{k}=\kappa$ with value $\frac{\kappa}{4}$. 
\item When $\kappa$ is small this will go up 
\item It's a finite sample effect, which scales down with $\frac{1}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}$
\end{itemize}
\item 3rd term is about the noise
\begin{itemize}
\item it's a finite sample effect, it scales down with $\frac{1}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}\bigl(\kappa\bigr)}$
and vanish when $n\to\infty$. 
\item its anisotropic factor is also $\frac{\lambda_{k}}{(\kappa+\lambda_{k})^{2}}$
so it will peak at $\lambda_{k}$with value $\frac{1}{4\kappa}$. 
\item When $\kappa$ is small this will keep go up and explode! 
\end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Accentuation error and OOD generalization}

\paragraph{Generalization error on the true distribution}

Normal on manifold test error reads 
\[
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta,\mathbf{x}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\Sigma)}\Big[((\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*})^{\top}\mathbf{x})^{2}\Big]
\]

This OOD generalization can be easily calculated via the deterministic
equivalence above. 

\begin{align*}
 & \mathbb{E}_{X,\eta,\mathbf{x}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\Sigma)}\Big[((\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*})^{\top}\mathbf{x})^{2}\Big]\\
= & \mathbb{E}_{X,\eta,\mathbf{x}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\Sigma)}\Big[(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*})^{\top}\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*})\Big]\\
= & \mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*})^{\top}\Sigma(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*})\Big]\\
= & \sum_{k=1}^{d}\lambda_{k}\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[(\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}\Big]
\end{align*}


\paragraph{Generalization error on the accentuation path}

Let's note that this is also the expected MSE error against the ground
truth, when we ``accentuate'' along a PC of data $\mathbf{x}=\alpha\mathbf{u}_{k}$,
then error reads, 
\[
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[(\mathbf{u}_{k}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}\Big]
\]

if we accentuate along $\hat{\beta}$ the error will be like
\[
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[(\hat{\beta}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}\Big]
\]

But there will be a scaling term to be determined. 

\subparagraph{Normalizer of the scaling.}

We have normalizer the normal response range is 
\[
\mathrm{Var}[\mathbf{x}^{\top}\beta^{*}]=\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\Sigma)}\Big[(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\beta^{*})^{2}\Big]=\beta^{*\top}\Sigma\beta^{*}
\]

Using accentuations to match this range, i.e. if we sample $\mathbf{x}=\alpha\hat{\beta}$,
let $\mathbb{E}[\alpha]=0$ be centered, then the predicted variance
is 
\[
\mathrm{Var}[\mathbf{x}^{\top}\hat{\beta}]=\mathrm{Var}[\alpha\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}]=(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta})^{2}\mathrm{Var}[\alpha]
\]

So if one want to match the variance of accentuation to that of the
natural variations, then 
\begin{align*}
(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta})^{2}\mathrm{Var}[\alpha] & =\beta^{*\top}\Sigma\beta^{*}\\
\mathrm{Var}[\alpha] & =\frac{\beta^{*\top}\Sigma\beta^{*}}{(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta})^{2}}
\end{align*}

Notably the ``accentuation evaluation error'' should actually be
\begin{align*}
 & \mathbb{E}_{X,\eta,\alpha}\Big[(\alpha\hat{\beta}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}\Big]\\
= & \mathbb{E}_{X,\eta,\alpha}\Big[\alpha^{2}(\hat{\beta}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}\Big]\\
= & \mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\mathrm{Var}[\alpha](\hat{\beta}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}\Big]\\
= & \mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\beta^{*\top}\Sigma\beta^{*}\frac{(\hat{\beta}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}}{(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta})^{2}}\Big]\\
= & \beta^{*\top}\Sigma\beta^{*}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\frac{(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}-\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*})^{2}}{(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta})^{2}}\Big]\\
= & \beta^{*\top}\Sigma\beta^{*}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\frac{(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta})^{2}-2\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}+(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*})^{2}}{(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta})^{2}}\Big]\\
= & \beta^{*\top}\Sigma\beta^{*}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\Big(1-2\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}+\frac{(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*})^{2}}{(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta})^{2}}\Big)\Big]\\
= & \beta^{*\top}\Sigma\beta^{*}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[1-2\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}+\Big(\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}\Big)^{2}\Big]
\end{align*}

Using the definition
\[
\hat{\beta}=(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}\beta^{*}+\sigma(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)
\]

The key quantity of analysis is 
\[
\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}
\]

Expanding it, 
\begin{align*}
\mathbf{\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}=} & \frac{\beta^{*\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}\beta^{*}+\sigma\beta^{*\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)}{\big((\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}\beta^{*}+\sigma(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)\big)^{\top}\big((\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}\beta^{*}+\sigma(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)\big)}\\
= & \frac{\beta^{*\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}\beta^{*}+\sigma\beta^{*\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)}{\beta^{*\top}\hat{\Sigma}^{2}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-2}\beta^{*}+2\sigma\beta^{*\top}\hat{\Sigma}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-2}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)+\sigma^{2}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-2}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)}
\end{align*}

Lets do the approximation i.e. 
\[
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}\Big]\approx\frac{\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}\Big]}{\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}\Big]}
\]

The approximation error is missing the covariance between the numerator
and denominator. 

Both numerator and denominator concentrates, 

The right hand side is much easier to compute 
\begin{align*}
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}\Big] & =\mathbb{E}_{X}\Big[\beta^{*\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-1}\hat{\Sigma}\beta^{*}\Big]\\
 & \asymp\beta^{*\top}(\Sigma+\kappa(\lambda)I_{d})^{-1}\Sigma\beta^{*}
\end{align*}
\begin{align*}
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}\Big] & =\mathbb{E}_{X}\Big[\beta^{*\top}\hat{\Sigma}^{2}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-2}\beta^{*}\Big]+\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\sigma^{2}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)^{\top}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-2}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta)\Big]\\
 & =\mathbb{E}_{X}\Big[\beta^{*\top}\hat{\Sigma}^{2}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-2}\beta^{*}\Big]+\sigma^{2}\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\mathrm{Tr}\Big[\frac{1}{n}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-2}(\frac{1}{n}X^{\top}\eta\eta^{\top}X)\Big]\\
 & =\mathbb{E}_{X}\Big[\beta^{*\top}\hat{\Sigma}^{2}(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-2}\beta^{*}\Big]+\frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}\mathbb{E}_{X}\mathrm{Tr}\Big[(\hat{\Sigma}+\lambda I_{d})^{-2}\hat{\Sigma}\Big]
\end{align*}

Both term can be tackled by the two point deterministic equivalence
or their derivatives. 

The 3rd term reads
\[
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\Big(\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}\Big)^{2}\Big]\approx\frac{\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*})^{2}\Big]}{\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[(\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta})^{2}\Big]}
\]

though doing the numerator and denominator is a bit complex, we may
approximate 
\[
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\Big(\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}\Big)^{2}\Big]\approx\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}\Big]^{2}\approx\Big(\frac{\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}\Big]}{\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}\Big]}\Big)^{2}
\]

Denote
\[
\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}\Big]\asymp R_{det}
\]

Then we can say the error reads,
\begin{align*}
 & \mathbb{E}_{X,\eta,\alpha}\Big[(\alpha\hat{\beta}^{\top}(\hat{\beta}-\beta^{*}))^{2}\Big]\\
 & =\beta^{*\top}\Sigma\beta^{*}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{X,\eta}\Big[1-2\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}+\Big(\frac{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\beta^{*}}{\hat{\beta}^{\top}\hat{\beta}}\Big)^{2}\Big]\\
 & \asymp\beta^{*\top}\Sigma\beta^{*}\cdot(1-R_{det})^{2}
\end{align*}

Error is on the (1/n) order. 
\noindent\fbox{\begin{minipage}[t]{1\columnwidth - 2\fboxsep - 2\fboxrule}%
In our previous notation convention, the equivalence reads
\[
A*B(\lambda+A*B)^{-1}MA*B(\lambda'+A*B)^{-1}\simeq T_{A}MT'_{A}+\kappa\kappa'G_{A}AG'_{A}\frac{\,\mbox{Tr}\left[AG_{A}MG'_{A}\right]}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}(\kappa,\kappa')}.
\]

with data dimension $d$, number of point $n$, with unnormalized
trace and unnormalized $\mathrm{df}_{2}\in[0,d]$, and denote the
$q=d/n$, where $n$ is the data point number 
As an immediate outcome, set $A=\Sigma$, $B=\frac{1}{n}XX^{T}$ as
white Wishart, then $A*B=\hat{\Sigma}$. Thus, 
\[
\hat{\Sigma}(\lambda+\hat{\Sigma})^{-1}M\hat{\Sigma}(\lambda^{\prime}+\hat{\Sigma})^{-1}\simeq T_{\Sigma}MT_{\Sigma}^{\prime}+\kappa\kappa'G_{\Sigma}\Sigma G_{\Sigma}^{\prime}\frac{\mbox{Tr}\left[G_{\Sigma}^{\prime}\Sigma G_{\Sigma}M\right]}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}(\kappa,\kappa')}
\]

in trace form we can write 
\[
\mbox{Tr}\Big[A\hat{\Sigma}(\lambda+\hat{\Sigma})^{-1}B\hat{\Sigma}(\lambda^{\prime}+\hat{\Sigma})^{-1}\Big]\simeq\mbox{Tr}\big[AT_{\Sigma}BT_{\Sigma}^{\prime}\big]+\frac{\kappa\kappa'}{n-\mathrm{df}_{2}(\kappa,\kappa')}\mbox{Tr}\big[AG_{\Sigma}\Sigma G_{\Sigma}^{\prime}\big]\mbox{Tr}\big[G_{\Sigma}^{\prime}\Sigma G_{\Sigma}B\big]
\]
%
\end{minipage}}

\[
w_{OLS}^{*}=\sigma_{y}\sum_{k=1}^{d}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_{k}}}\mbox{corr}\bigl(\mathbf{u}_{k}^{T}X,\,y\bigr)\,\mathbf{u}_{k}
\]

\begin{align*}
w_{Ridge}^{*} & =(\Sigma_{xx}+\lambda I)^{-1}\,\Sigma_{xy}\\[6pt]
 & =\sum_{k}\frac{1}{\lambda_{k}+\lambda}\,(\mathbf{u}_{k}^{T}\,\Sigma_{xy})\,\mathbf{u}_{k}\\[6pt]
w_{Ridge}^{*} & =\sigma_{y}\sum_{k}\frac{\sqrt{\lambda_{k}}}{\lambda_{k}+\lambda}\ \mbox{corr}\bigl(\mathbf{u}_{k}^{T}X,\,y\bigr)\,\mathbf{u}_{k}
\end{align*}

\end{document}
